content.text: Answer four problems. (If you turn in more, the first four will be graded.) Within reason, you may quote theorems as long as you state them clearly.

problem.text: Prove the following version of the Chinese remainder theorem: Let \(R\) be a commutative ring with \(1\) and let \(I, J\) be ideals of \(R\) such that \(I+J=R\). Then \(IJ=I\cap J\) and there is a ring isomorphism \(R/IJ\to (R/I)\times(R/J)\).
Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be a maximal ideal of \(R\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that if \(R\) has an identity then \(R/M\) is a field.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Give an example of a commutative ring \(R\) and a maximal ideal \(M\) of \(R\) such that \(R/M\) is not a field.

problem.text: Let \(R\) be a commutative ring with identity \(1\) different from \(0\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define what is a prime ideal of \(R\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Define what is a minimal prime ideal of \(R\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that \(R\) has a minimal prime ideal.

problem.text: Prove that the ring \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-2}]=\{a+b\sqrt{-2}:a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) is a Euclidean domain with respect to the norm defined by \(N(a+b\sqrt{-2})=a^2+2b^2\) for all \(a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\).

Determine the conjugacy classes of elements of order \(8\) in \(GL(5,\mathbb{Q})\), and, for each conjugacy class, give a representative matrix. (You may use the fact that \(x^4+1\) is irreducible in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\).)
Let \(F=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2},\sqrt{-1})\). Calculate the degree of the extension \(F/\mathbb{Q}\), and justify each of the steps of your calculation.
